title: Why does waiting change how much we value something?
summary: Explain why the things we wait for often feel more valuable than the things we get instantly, and consider what that reveals about value itself.
topic: How waiting changes the value of what we eventually receive
prompt: Write a 450–600-word essay explaining why waiting for something tends to change how much we value it. Use at least two concrete examples, acknowledge one case where waiting adds nothing, and use cleft sentences, the passive, and hedged generalisation.
hintStarters: It is not the object itself that gains value while we wait, but…
What anticipation does to a thing is, in a sense,…
Granted, there are forms of waiting that add nothing and only…
The reason a delayed reward often feels richer is that…

sự trì hoãn thỏa mãn

sự trì hoãn không cần thiết
làm nguội đi sự nhiệt tình

sampleText:
A friend once told me that the best meal of his life was a bowl of plain noodles eaten at two in the morning after a thirty-hour train journey across Kazakhstan. He has eaten in far finer places since. None of them, he insists, came close. The noodles were not remarkable. What had changed was everything around them — the hunger, the exhaustion, the long, swaying hours of having nothing to do but imagine the next stop.

This is the strange arithmetic of waiting. It is not the object itself that gains value while we wait, but our relationship to it. The bowl of noodles did not improve on the train. My friend did. Anticipation does something quiet to a person: it clears a space, builds a kind of psychological weight around the thing being awaited, so that when the thing finally arrives it lands on prepared ground. A reward that is earned through patience is, in some sense, partly made of the patience itself.

Consider how differently two children experience the same toy. One is handed it the moment it is mentioned; the other is told to wait until a birthday three weeks away. For the first child the toy is simply there, like the floor or the weather. For the second it has been imagined, dreamed about, half-built in the mind long before it is touched. When the wrapping comes off, the second child is not only receiving a toy — they are completing a story they have been telling themselves. The object carries an acquired meaning the first toy never had a chance to gather.

The same logic explains why fruit picked in season tastes better than fruit available all year. Part of the pleasure of a ripe mango in June is that you could not have had one in February. Scarcity and time conspire to heighten the experience. When something is always available, it tends to be quietly taken for granted; when it is rationed by the calendar, each instance is savoured as an event.

Granted, there are forms of waiting that add nothing at all. A flight delayed three hours on a runway does not make the destination sweeter; it only drains the traveller. A parcel held up by a careless courier does not ripen — it merely frustrates. What separates fruitful waiting from needless delay is, it seems, whether the waiting is filled with anticipation or only with annoyance. Time spent imagining a reward enriches it; time spent staring at a frozen progress bar does not. The first is an investment; the second is a theft.

This matters more now than it once did, because so much of modern life is engineered to compress the gap between wanting and having. A film, a meal, a conversation with someone far away — all can be summoned in seconds. The convenience is genuine and worth defending. Yet something is quietly lost when every desire is met before it has had time to deepen. What instant access offers in speed it may take away in meaning. The thing that arrives the moment we want it rarely gets the chance to become the thing we will remember.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'It is not … that …' cleft to locate the cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is not the object itself that gains value while we wait, but our relationship to it' dùng cấu trúc cleft 'It is not X that …, but Y' để chỉ chính xác điều gì thực sự thay đổi, bác bỏ một cách hiểu sai và thay bằng cách hiểu đúng. Đây là công cụ lập luận mạnh ở trình độ B2.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is not the object itself that gains value while we wait, but our relationship to it' uses an 'It is not X that …, but Y' cleft to pinpoint what really changes, rejecting one reading and substituting the correct one — a strong B2 argumentative move.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive + hedging for careful generalisation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'When something is always available, it tends to be quietly taken for granted' kết hợp thể bị động ('be taken for granted') với hedging ('tends to') để nêu một quy luật chung mà không tuyệt đối hóa. 'A reward that is earned through patience is, in some sense, partly made of …' cũng dùng 'in some sense' và 'partly' để giảm nhẹ khẳng định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'When something is always available, it tends to be quietly taken for granted' pairs the passive ('be taken for granted') with hedging ('tends to') to state a general rule without over-claiming. 'A reward … is, in some sense, partly made of the patience itself' likewise softens with 'in some sense' and 'partly'.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một người bạn từng kể với tôi rằng bữa ăn ngon nhất đời anh là một bát mì trắng ăn lúc hai giờ sáng sau một hành trình tàu hỏa ba mươi tiếng băng qua Kazakhstan. Kể từ đó anh đã ăn ở những nơi sang trọng hơn nhiều. Anh khăng khăng rằng chẳng nơi nào sánh được. Bát mì chẳng có gì đặc biệt. Thứ đã thay đổi là mọi thứ xung quanh chúng — cơn đói, sự kiệt sức, những giờ dài lắc lư chẳng có gì để làm ngoài việc tưởng tượng về điểm dừng tiếp theo.

Đây là phép tính kỳ lạ của sự chờ đợi. Không phải bản thân đồ vật tăng giá trị khi ta chờ đợi, mà là mối quan hệ của ta với nó. Bát mì không khá lên trên chuyến tàu. Bạn tôi mới là người khá lên. Sự mong đợi làm một điều lặng lẽ với con người: nó dọn ra một khoảng trống, xây nên một thứ trọng lượng tâm lý quanh thứ đang được chờ đợi, để khi thứ đó cuối cùng đến, nó rơi xuống mảnh đất đã được chuẩn bị sẵn. Một phần thưởng giành được nhờ sự kiên nhẫn, theo một nghĩa nào đó, được tạo nên một phần từ chính sự kiên nhẫn ấy.

Hãy xem hai đứa trẻ trải nghiệm cùng một món đồ chơi khác nhau ra sao. Một đứa được đưa món đồ ngay khi nó vừa được nhắc tới; đứa kia bị bảo phải đợi đến sinh nhật ba tuần sau. Với đứa thứ nhất, món đồ chơi đơn giản là ở đó, như sàn nhà hay thời tiết. Với đứa thứ hai, nó đã được hình dung, mơ tới, dựng lên một nửa trong tâm trí từ lâu trước khi được chạm vào. Khi lớp giấy gói được mở ra, đứa trẻ thứ hai không chỉ đang nhận một món đồ chơi — nó đang hoàn tất một câu chuyện mà nó đã kể cho chính mình. Món đồ vật mang một ý nghĩa được tích lũy mà món đồ chơi thứ nhất chẳng bao giờ có cơ hội gom góp.

Cùng một logic giải thích vì sao trái cây hái đúng mùa ngon hơn trái cây có quanh năm. Một phần khoái cảm của một quả xoài chín vào tháng Sáu là ở chỗ bạn đã không thể có nó vào tháng Hai. Sự khan hiếm và thời gian cùng nhau làm tăng thêm trải nghiệm. Khi một thứ luôn có sẵn, nó có xu hướng bị xem nhẹ một cách lặng lẽ; khi nó bị định lượng bởi lịch, mỗi lần đều được thưởng thức như một sự kiện.

Công nhận, có những kiểu chờ đợi chẳng thêm vào điều gì cả. Một chuyến bay bị hoãn ba tiếng trên đường băng không làm điểm đến ngọt ngào hơn; nó chỉ rút cạn sức người hành khách. Một bưu kiện bị một nhân viên giao hàng cẩu thả giữ lại thì không chín muồi — nó chỉ gây bực bội. Điều phân biệt sự chờ đợi có ích với sự trì hoãn không cần thiết, có vẻ như, là việc thời gian chờ đợi được lấp đầy bằng sự mong đợi hay chỉ bằng sự khó chịu. Thời gian dùng để tưởng tượng về một phần thưởng làm giàu cho nó; thời gian dùng để nhìn chằm chằm vào một thanh tiến trình đứng yên thì không. Cái thứ nhất là một sự đầu tư; cái thứ hai là một sự đánh cắp.

Điều này quan trọng hơn bây giờ so với trước kia, bởi rất nhiều phần của đời sống hiện đại được thiết kế để rút ngắn khoảng cách giữa mong muốn và sở hữu. Một bộ phim, một bữa ăn, một cuộc trò chuyện với người ở xa — tất cả đều có thể được triệu hồi trong vài giây. Sự tiện lợi là có thật và đáng được bảo vệ. Thế nhưng có một thứ gì đó lặng lẽ mất đi khi mọi ham muốn được đáp ứng trước khi nó kịp đào sâu. Những gì sự tiếp cận tức thì mang lại về tốc độ thì nó có thể lấy đi về ý nghĩa. Thứ đến ngay khoảnh khắc ta muốn nó hiếm khi có cơ hội trở thành thứ ta sẽ nhớ mãi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an 'It is not … that …, but …' cleft to locate what really changes?
options: It is not the object itself that gains value while we wait, but our relationship to it.
The object itself is not gaining value, our relationship gains it while we wait.
While we wait, gaining value is what the object and our relationship do together.
Our relationship and the object both that gain value while we wait it is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is not X that …, but Y' is the cleft that rejects one cause and substitutes another, pinpointing where the change occurs.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is not … that …' followed by 'but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces the case where waiting adds nothing, in a fitting register?
options: Honestly waiting is sometimes just super annoying and pointless, full stop.
Granted, there are forms of waiting that add nothing at all.
But also waiting can be bad too and everybody knows that already.
On the contrary, waiting is never ever a good thing in any situation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' concedes the limiting case cleanly and precisely without slang or absolutes.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses a formal concession opener and avoids overstatement?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses the passive with hedging to state a general tendency?
options: Available things always get completely taken for granted by everyone.
When something is always available, it tends to be quietly taken for granted.
People they take for granted always things that are available all time.
Taking things for granted is what always happens when available something is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It tends to be quietly taken for granted' combines the passive with 'tends to', stating a pattern without over-claiming.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option softens 'always' and puts the thing, not people, in subject position?

options: Refusing a reward forever
Putting off a pleasure now to gain a greater or richer one later
Receiving a reward immediately on request
Sharing a reward with others
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Delayed gratification' is the act of postponing immediate satisfaction in favour of a later, often greater, reward.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why did the bowl of noodles feel like the best meal?
options: Because it was cooked by a famous chef
Because the hunger, exhaustion and long wait had changed the eater, not the food
Because the noodles were unusually expensive
Because it was eaten with close friends
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the noodles did not improve; 'What had changed was everything around them' — the wait and the hunger.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread what the essay says actually changed about the meal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option keeps a consistent, formal explanatory register?
options: Waiting is honestly the best and instant stuff is just lame, no offence.
Scarcity and time conspire to heighten the experience.
Stuff you wait for is way better, that's basically just facts.
Things get good when you wait, super good, like really really good.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise vocabulary ('scarcity', 'conspire', 'heighten') and avoids slang and intensifiers.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'honestly', 'stuff', 'super' — choose the measured, precise sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: What distinguishes 'fruitful waiting' from 'needless delay' in the essay?
options: Whether the wait is short or long
Whether the waiting is filled with anticipation or only with annoyance
Whether the reward is expensive or cheap
Whether other people are also waiting
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states the difference is 'whether the waiting is filled with anticipation or only with annoyance.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the sentence that separates the two kinds of waiting.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to contrast a gain with a loss?
options: What instant access offers in speed it may take away in meaning.
Instant access offering speed is what it takes meaning away from.
In speed and meaning what instant access it offers and takes away.
It may take meaning away what instant access offers in speed of.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What instant access offers in speed it may take away in meaning' is a what-cleft balancing a gain against a loss.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'What … offers … it may take away …' balanced structure.

options: To rush through something quickly
To enjoy something slowly and fully
To save something for later
To complain about something
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Savour' means to take time to enjoy something deeply, drawing out the pleasure.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of slowly enjoying a taste rather than gulping it down.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best links the toy example back to the main claim?
options: The object carries an acquired meaning the first toy never had a chance to gather.
Toys, two children, weeks, birthdays, wrapping, the floor and weather.
And also there were two children and one of them got a toy, by the way.
Children toy birthday three weeks wait dreamed about it the second child.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A is a fluent sentence that connects the example to the essay's idea of meaning accumulating through waiting.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option is a complete, cohesive sentence rather than a list of fragments?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to compress the gap between wanting and having' mean?
options: To make objects physically smaller
To shorten the time between desiring something and getting it
To increase the price of things people want
To stop people from wanting things
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compress the gap' means to shrink the delay so that wanting and having happen almost at once.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'compress' as shortening, and the 'gap' as the waiting time.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence hedges a strong claim appropriately for B2 argument?
options: Instant access has absolutely destroyed all meaning in human life forever.
Something is quietly lost when every desire is met before it has had time to deepen.
Nobody who gets things instantly can ever value anything at all, period.
If you wait for nothing then your whole life becomes completely empty.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B makes a serious claim but qualifies it ('something', 'quietly', 'before it has had time'), avoiding absolutes.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to take something for granted' mean?
options: To pay extra attention to something
To value something so little that you stop noticing it
To give something away as a gift
To wait patiently for something
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To take for granted' is to assume something is always there and stop appreciating it.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of how you treat something that is always available.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure correctly to contrast investment and theft?
options: The first is an investment; the second is a theft.
The first investing and the second is stealing it from you sometimes.
Investment is first and a theft the second of them being two things.
First an investment, then a theft, which is the second one being it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The first is X; the second is Y' is a clean parallel structure that sharpens the contrast between the two kinds of waiting.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the balanced 'The first is …; the second is …' pattern.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's overall position on instant access?
options: It is purely harmful and should be abolished
Its convenience is real and worth defending, yet it can cost the meaning that waiting builds
It is always better than waiting in every case
It has no effect on how we value things
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay calls the convenience 'genuine and worth defending' but warns that meaning is lost when desire has no time to deepen.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph, which both defends and questions instant access.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of something you waited a long time for and something you got instantly. Which do you remember more vividly, and does the essay's idea — that waiting can become part of the reward — match your own experience or contradict it?
